title: Cardiovascular physiology: Cardiac action potential
body_html:
Cardiac action potential

Ventricular myocyte depolarisation. Click on image to enlarge

Note the relationship of depolarisation to muscle contraction. Na+ influx causes the initial depolarisation but it is the influx of Ca2+ that leads to muscle contraction towards the end of phase 2

PhaseDescriptionMechanism
0Rapid depolarisationRapid sodium influx
These channels automatically deactivate after a few ms
1Early repolarisationEfflux of potassium
2PlateauSlow influx of calcium
3Final repolarisationEfflux of potassium
4Restoration of ionic concentrationsResting potential is restored by Na+/K+ ATPase
There is slow entry of Na+ into the cell decreasing the potential difference until the threshold potential is reached, triggering a new action potential

NB cardiac muscle remains contracted 10-15 times longer than skeletal muscle

Conduction velocity

SiteSpeed
Atrial conductionSpreads along ordinary atrial myocardial fibres at 1 m/sec
AV node conduction0.05 m/sec
Ventricular conductionPurkinje fibres are of large diameter and achieve velocities of 2-4 m/sec (this allows a rapid and coordinated contraction of the ventricles

The SA node
Note that action potentials are not the same in all myocytes. Atrial myocytes have similar graphs to ventricular myocytes (as shown above) but the atrial action potential is shorter than the ventricular action potential. The plateau phase in the atrial action potential is also at a lower potential than that seen in the ventricle.

Pacemaker cells have more significant differences. The SA node lacks fast Na channels. Depolarisation of the SA node myocytes is via influx of Ca2+ ions via slow Ca2+ channels (rather than influx of sodium via fast Na channels). This results in slower depolarisation.

SA pacemaker depolarisation. Click on image to enlarge


Summary of resting and threshold potentials for pacemaker and non-pacemaker cells
SiteResting potentialThreshold potential
SAN-60mV-40mV
Ventricular and atrial myocytes-90mV-70mV
2
2
